questions.question: Which of the following is the primary reason a compiler often cannot auto-vectorise a simple loop like `for (int i = 0; i < N; i++) c[i] = a[i] + b[i];`?
options: The loop uses floating-point types
The compiler cannot prove that the pointers `a`, `b`, `c` do not alias (overlap in memory)
The loop body is too short
Floating-point addition is not associative
questions.explanation: When the compiler cannot prove that `a`, `b`, and `c` point to disjoint memory regions, it must assume the worst case (aliasing, where a write to `c[i]` could change `a[j]` or `b[j]`). This prevents vectorisation because vectorised code reads multiple elements before writing, which would produce wrong results under aliasing. Adding `__restrict__` tells the compiler the pointers are disjoint and allows vectorisation.

questions.question: What is the vector width (in bits) of an AVX2 register on x86-64?
options: 128 bits
256 bits
512 bits
64 bits
questions.explanation: AVX2 uses 256-bit YMM registers. SSE uses 128-bit XMM registers, and AVX-512 uses 512-bit ZMM registers. Each 256-bit register can hold 8 single-precision (f32) or 4 double-precision (f64) values.

questions.question: After running the AVX2 dot-product kernel on two arrays of 1 million floats, you measure a 4× speedup over scalar code. AVX2 can execute 8 float operations per instruction. Why is the speedup less than 8×?
options: The compiler incorrectly optimised the scalar code
Horizontal reduction (summing the 8 vector lanes into one scalar) adds overhead, and memory bandwidth may be a bottleneck
AVX2 cannot process more than 4 floats at once for multiplications
The `_mm256_load_ps` intrinsic requires 64-byte alignment and causes a fault otherwise
questions.explanation: The dot product requires a horizontal reduction at the end to combine the 8 partial sums into a single scalar. This reduction involves extracting the upper 128 bits, adding pairs, and performing two `_mm256_hadd_ps` operations — all of which are serialising steps that do not achieve 8× throughput. Additionally, if the data does not fit in L1/L2 cache, memory bandwidth may become the limiting factor. An 8× speedup is only achievable for purely vertical (lane-wise) operations on data that stays in registers.

questions.question: You are writing a cross-platform SIMD library that must run on both x86-64 (AVX2) and AArch64 (NEON). Which approach is most maintainable?
options: Write two separate implementations using platform-specific intrinsics (`<immintrin.h>` and `<arm_neon.h>`) guarded by `#ifdef`
Use Rust's portable SIMD (`std::simd::f32x8`) so the same source compiles to efficient code on both ISAs
Use the `-mavx2` flag on both platforms and rely on the compiler to translate x86 intrinsics to NEON
Write scalar code only; modern compilers auto-vectorise well enough on both platforms
questions.explanation: Rust's `std::simd` provides a portable abstraction: `f32x8` compiles to AVX2 on x86 and to paired NEON 128-bit operations on AArch64. Option 0 (platform intrinsics) works but duplicates code and is error-prone. Option 2 is impossible — x86 intrinsics have no meaning on ARM. Option 3 (relying purely on auto-vectorisation) gives up control and often leaves performance on the table. A C++ alternative would be the Highway library or `std::experimental::simd`.

questions.question: What is the main benefit of SIMD programming over scalar programming?
options: SIMD code is always shorter
SIMD processes multiple data elements with a single instruction, achieving data-level parallelism
SIMD eliminates the need for multithreading
SIMD works only on GPUs
questions.explanation: SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) instructions operate on vectors — e.g., AVX-256 adds 8 floats in one cycle vs 8 scalar additions. This is independent of threading (you can have SIMD on each thread). It's ideal for numerical computing, image processing, and any loop over arrays.
